title: Re-Engaging with Life After Loss
subtitle: A daily practice for reconnecting with the living world, one thing at a time
excerpt: A brief daily activity for re-engaging with the living world after a period of grief. The practice asks you to notice one thing each day that connects you to what is still here. Small and repeatable.
sourceNotes: Draws on behavioural activation principles from grief and bereavement research. Standard approach in post-loss recovery work.

practice.whenToUse: Use when grief has made it hard to feel connected to daily life. Not for acute grief — for the longer period when you are re-learning how to be present in your days.

content.text: A brief daily practice for re-engaging with the living world after a period of loss. Grief narrows attention toward absence. This practice points attention, briefly and deliberately, at presence.
New to activities as a practice? Read
Once each day (morning, midday, or evening, whichever fits), pause for a moment.
Notice one thing that connects you to the living world. Something present, not past. A sound outside, a texture, the temperature of the air, something growing, the weight of a cup in your hand.
Stay with it for ten to twenty seconds. That is the whole practice.

The practice does not ask you to feel better about the loss. It asks you to notice, briefly, that life is still continuing alongside it. Do it on the days you cannot feel it as much as on the days it comes more easily.
Draws on grief research and behavioural activation principles.
